8 min read / 1h3m video / 2h8m audio feedback
0:00 / 7:29
This lesson is all about creating a compelling entry point into
your world using a 500 — 800 word Facebook ad.
Start by watching this one-hour presentation. Shawn explains
how to think about Facebook ads from a perspective we’ve never
shared publicly before.
Then he discusses a way to create an authentic, conversational
Facebook ad that doesn’t feel like an ad.
Two things before you watch the video:
First, Shawn mentions the importance of the ‘hook’ in a Facebook
ad early and he forgot to mention it again. (That’s one of the
dangers of recording these videos in a single take.)
You’ll �nd the relevant information about the importance of a
Facebook hook, and how to create them, excerpted from The
Traf�c Engine (below).
Second, a summary of your homework appears below as well. We
chose selections to review and share from the work submitted.
Lesson 3: Facebook Ad
2 Files
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 8 --

Facebook Ad Hooks (Excerpted
from The Tra�c Engine)
The hook is the �rst two lines of a Facebook ad. That’s my term — I’ve
heard that called by many names.
My two favorite hooks are dialogue and questions (which can be
combined too).
‘You’re never going to believe what my accountant told me about
the Payroll Protection Loan…’
‘I’m sorry Shawn, I talked to your attorney and there’s nothing we
can do to help…’
‘What if everything I believed about gaining muscle after turning
40 was wrong?’
‘Three things I wish every digital marketer knew about paid
advertising on Google and Facebook…’
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

The opportunities are endless.
The �rst two lines of an ad pre-qualify, so be careful about who and
how you attract. At the same time, remember that the �rst two lines
‘sell’ the rest of the ad. A little conversational curiosity to get a
prospect’s attention is OK, in my opinion, if the ad copy is
transparent.
Shawn’s ‘Rule of 3’
The Rule of 3 was �rst mentioned during a landing page review.
Shawn realized he hadn’t ever mentioned it to anyone — including
André!
The Rule of 3 can be used for everything you’ll write in this
workshop. It works at the micro-level (for example, what to
include in a single Facebook ad), and at the macro-level (for
example, your thematic arc might follow the Rule of 3).
Here’s an overview.
Enter Shawn…
(BTW, I’m sure I did not invent this idea — I just gave the
framework a name when I noticed how often I use it.)
Structure is important to me when I write. I want to know where
I’m going and the order in which I’ll get there.
I also want to know that my ideas �ow from one to the next, that
I’ve supported those ideas well, and that there’s a natural
progression from A to B to C.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 8 --

The Rule of 3 starts with a controlling idea / thesis statement (this
is the main point I’m making). Then, I make three main points
that are each accompanied by three supporting ideas.
Finally, I want to direct the reader (or viewer) to what’s next.
Structurally, I imagine it like this:
Controlling Idea / Thesis Statement
�. Main point #1.
�. Supporting idea #1.
�. Supporting idea #2.
�. Supporting idea #3.
�. Main point #2.
�. Supporting idea #1.
�. Supporting idea #2.
�. Supporting idea #3.
�. Main point #3.
�. Supporting idea #1.
�. Supporting idea #2.
�. Supporting idea #3.
Call to Action
This framework works for writing and editing.
For example, if you’re writing a 1,000-word Facebook ad, start with
the main takeaway you want the reader to believe (that’s your
controlling idea).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 8 --

Then, identify the three things that person needs to believe in
order to accept your controlling idea.
Make sure those ideas make sense and that they follow logically
from one to the other. (They’re not just random ideas thrown on
the page.)
Next, support each of those three main points with (up to) three
proof points.
Finally, after you’ve made your case (like a prosecutor), tell the
reader what to do next.
If you’re editing your work or another person’s work, map their
ideas to the ‘rule of 3’ structure. (This can be an eye-opening
exercise.)
▪ Is the controlling idea obvious?
▪ Do the main points �ow?
▪ Are they supported with evidence?
▪ Does the CTA lead naturally to what’s next?
I suspect some of you are wondering “does it have to be three
main points and three supporting ideas?”
No. These are guidelines. However, 3 x 3 works very well in most
circumstances. Judgment and discretion are required.
And yes, for those of you who are astute observers, the Rule of 3 is
fractal.
There are three parts (controlling idea, making the case, call to
action), and within each part are three additional parts.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 8 --

(If you’re writing much longer copy, you might include three sub-
points under each supporting idea, for example.)
It’s threes within threes within threes. (Our brains seem to
understand groups of three well.)
The key to this framework’s effectiveness is its simplicity. Don’t
overthink it!
Lesson 3 Homework
Write your own 500 — 800 word Facebook ad following the
guidelines from the screencast.
This ad is the entry point for prospects into your world, and it has
three jobs:
▪ Set the frame for the entire customer journey.
▪ Attract prospects who can become happy customers.
▪ Repel prospects who are unlikely to become happy
customers.
Do not overthink this exercise.
You are not writing prose, and you shouldn’t be thinking about
writing ‘ad copy.’ We want to avoid any tone that feels like copy /
advertising.
Write conversationally.
Instead, we’re writing from the heart by focusing on the needs of
our audience �rst, then setting them up for a journey into our
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 8 --

world through the lens of the frame we’ve established.
Three suggestions to keep Facebook happy (and improve your
results):
▪ Do not use “you”, “your”, or similar language that calls out a
person as an individual.
▪ Do not use numbers (especially as proof points). This can be
really tricky because you may want to include statements
like “I’ve worked with hundreds of people who…” Use your
discretion — just make sure you avoid making any outcome-
based claims (in general). For example, even if it’s 100% true I
would not say “I’ve helped hundreds of people grow their
revenue, on average, 65%”. (Avoiding bold claims altogether
is safer with Facebook.)
▪ Shorter sentences and shorter paragraphs are important.
More white spaces makes your writing easier to read.
Note: In the feedback recording below we refer to this as
‘Week 2,’ not Lesson 3. As we said at the start, we’ve retooled
the live workshop to now be a self-paced course.
Lesson 3 Feedback
We ended up reviewing four homework submissions that we felt
would be broadly valuable to everyone (their ads are in the comments
below).
▪ Shane Melanson
▪ Stacy
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 8 --

▪ Francis Waldecker
▪ Nick Warren
We evaluated the ads, we used the following questions (we encourage
you to use these questions yourself).
Facebook Ad review questions:
�. Do the �rst two sentences grab the prospect’s attention and
make him or her want to read more?
�. Does the copy communicate empathy and understanding by
focusing �rst on the prospects’ needs, desires, fears and
concerns?
�. Is there a rising and falling of tension that pulls the reader’s
attention through the ad from beginning to end?
�. Are the bene�ts from engaging with the writing obvious to the
reader?
�. Is the call to action interesting, compelling, and intriguing?
Here is our 2 hour 8 minute review of the work.

Post your questions below.
—André & Shawn
